response: The global food system faces unprecedented pressures as the world population is expected to reach 9.7 billion by 2050, driving a projected need for 60% more food production. Climate change is already lowering yields via droughts, floods, and extreme temperatures, while agriculture contributes about 25% of greenhouse gas emissions through deforestation, livestock methane, and fertilizer nitrous oxide; water scarcity further threatens high-yield regions such as the rapidly depleting Ogallala Aquifer. Soil degradation has reduced productivity of 33% of the world’s arable land, and food waste means about one-third of food produced is lost or wasted between farm and fork. Proposed solutions include precision agriculture (AI/sensors), vertical farming, alternative proteins, drought-resistant genetic modification, and regenerative farming, alongside policy changes—though the key challenge is scaling them affordably for the poorest populations.
